Name: rate_distortion_quantization
Title: Optimizing Neural Network Compression via Rate-Distortion Theory
Short Hypothesis: By framing neural network quantization and pruning as a rate-distortion problem, we can derive optimal compression strategies that minimize predictive distortion while satisfying information-theoretic bounds on model representation.
Related Work: Existing literature on rate-distortion bounds (e.g., Sefidgaran et al. 2022) shows a connection between information-theoretic principles and machine learning generalization. However, few works explicitly connect rate-distortion principles to adaptive quantization and pruning strategies in neural networks. This proposal distinguishes itself by unifying these concepts under a common theoretical framework, providing a rigorous exploration of achievable compression rates.
Abstract: In this work, we explore the optimization of neural network compression through the lens of rate-distortion theory. We frame quantization and pruning as a lossy source coding problem, aiming to minimize predictive distortion while adhering to information-theoretic bounds on model representation. Our approach involves deriving tight bounds on the bits-per-weight required to maintain predictive sufficiency and establishing a clear relationship between model complexity, quantization error, and generalization performance. Through a series of experiments, we will empirically validate our theoretical predictions, offering insights into optimal compression strategies for neural networks. By providing a principled understanding of the trade-offs involved in model compression, this research aims to advance the field of efficient machine learning while ensuring robustness and interpretability.
Experiments: 1. Develop a theoretical framework for deriving achievable rates and distortion bounds for various quantization and pruning strategies.
2. Implement adaptive quantization algorithms that dynamically adjust bit allocation based on the local sensitivity of model parameters to predictive performance.
3. Conduct experiments on standard benchmark datasets (e.g., CIFAR-10, ImageNet) to evaluate the impact of different quantization and pruning strategies on model accuracy and compression rates.
4. Measure the predictive distortion using task-relevant metrics and compare against existing compression methods.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The theoretical predictions may not fully align with empirical results, requiring further refinement of the models.
Existing quantization and pruning methods may limit the applicability of the proposed framework if they do not adhere to the derived principles.
The complexity of deriving tight bounds may lead to increased computational overhead in the experimental phase.
